name: SismoVzla
db_description:
# Páginas Amarillas: Sismo Vzla

**Páginas Amarillas: Sismo Vzla** es una plataforma web de contingencia y código abierto diseñada para centralizar, organizar y verificar herramientas de ayuda frente a la crisis sísmica en Venezuela. Ante la sobreinformación y dispersión de enlaces críticos que ocurre en situaciones de emergencia, este directorio actúa como un hub único, ágil y confiable para la ciudadanía.

### ✨ Características Principales:
*   **Directorio Centralizado:** Agrupa y clasifica bases de datos en tiempo real sobre desaparecidos, pacientes hospitalizados, centros de donación, refugios y mapas de riesgo.
*   **Búsqueda Inteligente (Algoritmo Flexible):** Implementación de un motor de búsqueda interno con *stemming* básico que permite a los usuarios encontrar recursos rápidamente, reconociendo raíces de palabras para tolerar errores tipográficos en momentos de estrés.
*   **Diseño Accesible (Estética Retro/Pixel-Art):** Interfaz limpia, de alto contraste y gamificada visualmente para reducir la carga cognitiva del usuario en momentos de crisis. 
*   **Internacionalización (i18n):** Soporte multilingüe nativo para facilitar el acceso a ONG y cuerpos de ayuda internacional.

Está construido bajo una arquitectura moderna y escalable utilizando:
*   **Frontend:** Next.js (React), TypeScript y Tailwind CSS.
*   **Infraestructura:** Despliegue en Vercel (Edge Network) con dominio propio (`sismove.info`) para garantizar alta disponibilidad ante picos de tráfico masivos.

architecture.pattern: Single Next.js App Router client app reading one static Firestore document via the Firebase JS SDK; no backend/API routes of its own
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Firebase Firestore client SDK (firebase/firestore) used directly from a client-side service module (getDoc on a single 'directory/all_sites' document); no server-side data layer, no ORM, no writes from the app
architecture.api_design: None — no REST/GraphQL endpoints; data fetched client-side directly from Firestore, cached in sessionStorage for 30 minutes
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable for its size: hooks/useSites.ts wraps services/sitesService.ts wraps lib/firebase.ts, with data/categories.ts and data/sites.ts as typed constants
architecture.modularity: Small, flat structure (components, hooks, services, lib, data, locales); everything fits in ~716 LOC
notable_patterns: Client-side session caching of Firestore reads
next-intl i18n middleware for es/en locales
Category-based client-side filtering with a custom fuzzy-match search function

code_organization.directory_structure: Clear conventional Next.js layout (src/app, components, hooks, services, lib, data, locales) appropriate for the app's small scope
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive naming (WebSiteItem, useSites, sitesService, CategoryId)
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is a compelling mission statement (origin story, how to contribute) but has zero technical/setup documentation (no env var list, no install/run instructions beyond implicit npm scripts)
code_organization.reasoning: Well-organized for its size but the codebase is extremely thin (~716 LOC total) — essentially a single-page directory listing app with almost no business logic.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Firebase config uses NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars (expected/acceptable for Firebase client SDK, but there is no .env.example in the repo and no documentation of required vars), .env*.local is gitignored
production_readiness.security_notes: No Firestore security rules are present in the repo (data is presumably admin-managed elsewhere), no authentication/authorization anywhere in the app, no rate limiting, no input validation beyond a client-side search filter — acceptable only because the app is read-only public content, but there is no visibility into how the single Firestore document is written/moderated
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Entire directory is stored as one Firestore document fetched wholesale on load; fine for tens/hundreds of entries but this pattern doesn't scale to large datasets or support server-side pagination/search
production_readiness.reasoning: Only a Dockerfile.dev (development-mode Docker, running `npm run dev`) exists — there is no production Dockerfile, no CI/CD workflow (the only .github file is CODEOWNERS), and no tests of any kind. Error handling exists (try/catch with a user-facing retry UI) but overall this is a lightly-productionized static content directory, not a robust system.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, working code (not generic AI boilerplate) — it's a genuine, functioning small app reportedly live at sismove.info — but the scope is minimal: a single searchable/filterable card grid pulling from one Firestore doc, no server logic, no tests, no CI.
maturity.reasoning: 25 commits, single contributor, no tests, no CI/CD pipeline, no production deployment config in-repo; README focuses on mission/community rather than engineering rigor.

viability.reasoning: It is a simple, low-risk, already-deployed link directory that genuinely helps route people to other resources (hospitals, missing-persons lists, donation centers) during the earthquake response, and its simplicity is itself a virtue for fast, reliable operation; however it provides no unique functionality beyond curating external links, has no tests/CI/production Docker config, and its usefulness is entirely dependent on the quality/freshness of a single manually-maintained Firestore document with no visible moderation or update workflow in the code.

key_features: Bilingual (es/en) categorized directory of contingency-related websites/resources (missing persons, hospitals, donations, pets, volunteering, maps)
Client-side fuzzy search and category filtering over the directory list
SessionStorage caching of Firestore reads (30 min TTL) to reduce reads
Retro/pixel-art themed responsive UI with Venezuela flag color branding
Loading/error states with a manual retry button on Firestore fetch failure
analysis.merge_potential: Best used as a lightweight, standalone landing/directory page (e.g., embedded or linked from a more feature-rich hub) rather than merged code-wise into another project, since it has no shared data model, no API, and no auth to integrate with other tools; its Firestore 'directory' document schema (WebSiteItem list) could however be adopted as a simple resource-linking convention by other Build4Venezuela projects.
red_flags: No automated tests of any kind
No CI/CD workflow (only a CODEOWNERS file under .github)
No production Dockerfile — only Dockerfile.dev running `next dev`
No Firestore security rules in-repo and no documentation of how the single 'directory/all_sites' document is authored/moderated, so data integrity/trust cannot be verified from the code
Single contributor, only 25 commits, project created 3 days before this review (2026-06-28)
analysis.summary: Sismo Vzla is a small, real, and already-live (sismove.info) bilingual Next.js directory app that curates and lets users search/filter external contingency resources (missing persons, hospitals, donation centers, volunteering, maps) for the Venezuela earthquake response. It reads a single Firestore document client-side via the Firebase SDK, has no backend, auth, tests, CI, or production Docker config, and is essentially a well-executed but very thin 'link aggregator' rather than a full-featured aid platform.

infra_deploy: implied Vercel/static hosting for sismove.info (no config in repo)

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as an open-source 'yellow pages' for the earthquake response — one bilingual link to find missing-persons lists, hospitals, donation centers, and risk maps instead of scattered social media posts. Hold the push until the live directory reliably loads content and has a visible, credible pipeline for who curates/verifies the linked resources.
gaps: Live demo currently shows only a 'Cargando directorio...' loading state with no visible content in an automated fetch — needs manual browser verification that Firestore data actually renders for end users
No demo video or screenshots to show the populated directory in action
No documented data moderation/update process, so donors/press can't be told how fresh or trustworthy the listed resources are
No tests, CI, or production deploy config — a single-document Firestore backend fetched wholesale is fragile at any real traffic spike

evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch on https://www.sismove.info/es rendered a page shell with the full category navigation (Ver Todo, Desaparecidos, Niños, Mascotas, Hospitales, Ayudas/Donaciones, Voluntariado, Denuncias, Mapas de Riesgo, Aportar) but the content area only showed "Cargando directorio..." (Loading directory...) with no directory entries visible. This may be a limitation of the fetch tool not executing the client-side Firebase/Firestore JS fetch (the app is architected as client-side-only data loading per the insight JSON), rather than proof the site is truly broken — but it means the automated check could not confirm the directory actually populates with real resource links. A manual browser check is needed before promoting.
evaluation.one_line_pitch: SismoVzla: las "páginas amarillas" del sismo — un solo hub bilingüe que centraliza desaparecidos, hospitales, refugios, donaciones y mapas de riesgo para no perderse en la avalancha de enlaces durante la emergencia.
evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: During a disaster, critical links (missing-persons registries, hospital status, donation centers, shelters, risk maps) get scattered across social media, WhatsApp forwards, and ad-hoc threads, making them hard to find and easy to lose amid misinformation. A single, bilingual, categorized, searchable directory that curates and verifies these links addresses a well-known and recurring pain point in emergency response (info fragmentation), and is genuinely useful to both victims and NGOs/international responders. However, the project itself is a thin link-aggregator (716 LOC, single Firestore document, no visible moderation workflow) rather than a feature-rich aid platform, so its real-world impact depends entirely on how well-populated and maintained the underlying resource list is — something not verifiable from the code or a quick live check.
